author: @TheKoodus
text:
Dear Robert, firstly thank you so  much for all the content you put out. I have no background in electronics or chemistry and I still find your content fascinating! 
When I saw your SILAR machine running just now I thought of a possible improvement; instead of having the head going round and dunking the piece in each jar, would this be possible? To have the head fixed in the centre with a motor to rotate it and another motor to raise and lower it. Then suspended off of the bottom two booms at 90 degrees to each other (making a cross). This way you could have work on 4 pieces at the same time instead of one. 
The head would lower (either lowering itself, or just the boom arm) and dunk the 4 pieces simultaneously for desired time. Then it would raise up so that the pieces were clear of the jars, and turn through 90 degrees so that each piece moved on to the next jar to be repeated. 
I guess the limitation is that the pieces could only be dipped for the same amount of time. If they needed to be dipped for different amounts of time, you could have the same cross-boom idea but with independent lowering motors on the ends of the boom. 
Anyway I hope you got the picture, and that it might at least provide food for thought ;) Hope you are doing OK through the current situation and thanks again for all you do!!

author: @RWBHere
text:
It's an interesting approach, Robert. Thank-you.
You could have a much simpler system to do the same thing (and without using an Arduino, replacing it with a small timer switching a thyristor), as follows:
# Use an up/down motor, controlled by a 555 timer (or a mechanical timer motor).
# Have four containers on a rotating turntable, which rotates a quarter turn before stopping.
# Drop the sample into beaker 1 as you are already doing, with stop switches at each end if the vertical travel. (Or simpler, 1 microswitch operated by 2 pins on each end of a sliding arm.)
# Pause for 30 seconds - or whatever time you choose (a 555 timer, or mechanical equivalent timing all motions in dipper arm and turntable)
# Raise the sample.
# Rotate the turntable gently by 90° using a motor stopped by a sensor switch (an ordinary micro switch working a relay) being operated by 4 pins on the turntable bottom
# Literally rinse and repeat ad infinitum.
# (Optional) Count the total number of cycles by using a spigot to click a mechanical counter once every full rotation. If you're posh, then you could use an electronic counter driven by a Hall Effect sensor, etc! If you prefer slumming it, then you could time one full cycle, and multiply by 500, to ascertain the stop time, without physically counting the number of turntable rotations.

You will need to refresh the rinse water containers on a fairly regular basis, as you are no doubt doing with your existing machine.

Apart from the great simplicity of electronics and simpler construction (a maximum of 3 micro switches, one vertical motion and one rotary motion), the machine will be tolerant of power interruptions, especially if you use a mains-powered rechargeable battery operated system, which could keep running for days during a prolonged power outage.

author: @energycrafts8206
text:
Rob.  Don't let anybody distract your design plan, including me.  Nothing wrong with "old school" mechanical relays and switches.  I worked at Kellogg's a 100 year old company (makes breakfast cereals and waffles).  There are tremendous ranges of tech approaches to moving waffles as example on a production line to different locations.  Even today while they do use modern  PLC controllers for motor position and rate, stop, start etc., there are still many processes that rely on simple limit switches, dpdt relays and timer modules.  Switches and relays (mechanical) are often rated in the area of a million cycles before failure. 

 I like simple and very reliable.  No need to get too complicated.  Some history.  Back before the PLCs were common, processes requiring a complex sequence of on/offs of multiple switch contacts used a rotary switch assembly.  It was simply a small gear motor rotating a very long shaft having small weight arms that were independently adjustable per a huge number of micro limit switches (Cherry) aligned along the long shaft.  So on/off or delay of some about is easy to achieve by the logic of the micro switch positions and if desired, using that logic to turn on/off the rotary motor per switch sequence to make/break continuity.  Simple logic functions of and/or/nor etc are so easy.  No software, no computer and accurate as needed.  

Nobody makes them anymore that I know of, but an old CNC we had used one in sequence logic controls.  When the motorized rotary switch finally died (20 years all day operating) we bought the last spare that the manufacturer had in stock.  But I think the equivalent could be easily fashioned from a mounted row of limit switches having a long threaded rod with either nuts or tiny arms on the rod arranged to strike the limit switch arms.  A lock nut holds the position of the tiny arms/nut positions.  The motor can start/stop ect. based on the limit switch tiny arms position that you "program".  The motor can run as simple short pulsed motions or more continuous all according to your switch "programming".  I only mention the pulse meaning you can control length of time between say LS1 closed and say LS2 open, and on to the next order of sequence.  In fact, some of the early CNC's did not use PLCs, rather they used what was known as a sequencer.  It was just a hardwired "canned" logic of all the general functions of a CNC, like home position, move x axis ect.  Of course your design won't need this much logic, it is good to know it can if desired.  For your heater, a simple thermistor or thermocouple and a temperature controller module with a digital readout is very nice and simple.  Also the controller can be set to open/close external devices per temp ramp you setup.  Done it for a conveyor belt running through a heating oven at work.  Thought this info may help in this or other projects.

author: @teamlight8567
text:
Robert are you aware of the work done by 'Ukraine's  Institute  For Problems of Material Siences? 
"At the  Institute  for Problems of Materials Science  in  L’vov,  Republic  of Ukraine, Academician Trefilov, a Senior Fellow of  the Russian Academy  of Sciences and  Vice-President of  Mathematics of  the Ukrainian Academy  of Sciences, headed up a  team which has produced a  solid-state,  non-chemical,  carbon-lattice  energy storage  device  called an  ‘energy  accumulator.’ 
The  mono-molecular  sheets deposited by  this technique are  wrapped back  and forth on top of each other, more than one  million times per millimeter, and are  separated from  each other by  a  distance  of less than one  atomic diameter. At this level of  construction, the material  becomes subject to the rules of  quantum  mechanics which are  almost entirely  probabilistic. That means a whole atom  of carbon  (or  almost anything  else except an electron or  photon)  will  not fit  in the  space  which separates the lattice  sheets.   When viewed under  an electron microscope, the sheets produce  a  pattern which looks for  all  the world like  an endless field of  four-sided pyramids, connected base  to base, on a  single plane, with the  tips of the  pyramids protruding  endlessly, uniformly  upwards. When wrapped back and  forth on  top of each other, these  sheets of pure  carbon  crystal, made  of carbon  molecules shaped like  trillions of identical tiny  pyramids, all arrayed endlessly  in identical formation, are  positioned so that the  tips of the  pyramids on the  bottom sheet are matched with the  tips of the  pyramids on the  top sheets. What remains between the pyramid  tips are  open ‘spaces’  or  energy  wells."  
Wonder if that could be done with your SILAR system?

author: @whisky_9o5
text:
You just build everything the old school garage door openers have, with limit switches and relays....except we used a small screw drive that would go between the switches! We would adjust the nuts on screw pending how far we wanted to travel in any direction and hit the Reed switches and reverse the travel. No Arduino, no stepper motors.

 When I visited my dad years ago he had a slew of them waiting for me to repair!  Usually stuck relays or bad switch. 
We're talking 30 or so.. they were in the field like 10+ years. He had modified them to gate openers for customers!  Every time I showed up on the island he went ...I got a "small job" for you!  I sit there for the day and repair them all... Loved them things. Fascinating stuff!

author: @excitedbox5705
text:
Stepper motors only cost a couple dollars each. I buy 600mm ballscrew and rail kits with motor for $26 each. A belt will be a lot cheaper but they are not as sturdy and wear out. But building a 600x600mm by 100mm setup would only cost about $70 with ballscrews and slide rails. 



The absolute cheapest and easiest would be to put the containers in a line and make it 2 axis with a clothesline type setup. A looped belt with an unpowered pulley on it will let you sling a rope over the pulley for up and down by winding a piece of string on a motor and horizontal movement by jogging the belt and the dipper motor at the same time. That would cost under $10 with 2 steppers ($4), 2x string (pennies), 3 plastic pulleys (10 cents), 5x scrap wood for a frame (free). dual stepper driver ($1.80), ESP32 ($2-4) and a few nails.

replies.author: @aga5897
replies.text:
3D printer heated bed is a great idea !
Stirring kinda comes down to 'agitation'.
Magnetic stirbars move about in a magnetic field, so they don't all need to be exactly centralised on a spinning magnet. 1 Motor with magnets on 1 bar, 20 beakers with stirbars, spin the motor, they all get some agitation.

replies.author: @aga5897
replies.text:
Rik rik Magentic stirbars just sit in the bottom of a beaker. You only need to Move them to create agitation in the water/solvent. A Magnet passing in the wrong place will not make them spin, but it will make them Move. Basically 1 motor, a big arm with several magnets that spins under the entire plate of 9 beakers will work, ish.
I did a video recently where the stir magnet is not correctly over the spinning magnet.
https://youtu.be/9vuUnbJth58?t=58
You can see the stirbar still causes 'agitation' despite being off-centre from the motor/magnet.

replies.text: unless you want to add heating mate and that's on the cards - the rotating arm is pretty hard to implenet as it happens - but there are a hundred ways to do this and I ain't saying i know what is for best - it's just a guess on my part

author: @googacct
text: Interesting project. A few other ideas for implementation  Adapt a cheap 3d printer gantry. The advantage is it could be programmed with g-code. Another option might be to use a toy robotic arm to move the sample between the different beakers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are lots of ways of doing this mate - but interestingly enough - most ideas never become reality - things seem simple until you try to make them

author: @gr1f1th

replies.text: you are quite right mate and a single run line where the beakers were in line would have also reduced the construction demand - but we just try and see

author: @Margarinetaylorgrease
text:
I used to maintain a 24track tape machine that used basically the same method to move the 2 pinch rollers into and out of place.

It is important to use a diode across the relay coil when solid state control is used to remove the back EMF problems.
Back EMF will destroy your transistor or Arduino output as the collapsing magnetic field can cause very high voltages.

I found out the hard way

replies.author: @Margarinetaylorgrease
replies.text: Rob, I'm sure you know all this but I thought I'd better say anyway as many don't. I also had a vision of some young, past me getting very frustrated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I won't be driving the relays directly from the arduino mate - i plan to use a relay shield to drive the DPDT relays - the shield should have the diodes on it - but nice point cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - it's no worries mate - i thought it was a really good point to make and appreciated it

author: @Killianwsh
text: Thanks Rob, Never heard of this tech. Sounds very interesting. Very much looking fwd. to learning more about it!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is pretty awesome mate

author: @tacrom
text:
Just a thought, have the Z axis rotate on a base plate, you containers are then placed around the rotating Z, only two motors and shitloads less extrusion required and save workbench area required.


Nice job though :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are a ton of ways to solve this problem mate - as pr i am trying what i think is the simplest possible - having 3 stages all driven the same way seems less complex than a rotating arm where a stepper would be needed - sure you can calculate position but you would never be sure unless you tracked it or home point or start end position - all of that need some sensor in play - maybe an opto band or wheel or hall sensor - what ever it seems to add complication which i am not sure is necessary when you think all we want is four positions and that can be held as a two by teo matrix read from switch position - that way the switches do two jobs and no further construction is needed - not say what you are saying is not good mate - just going through my reasoning

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
